changesProposed.name: Alumni Ventures

verified.name: Alumni Ventures
verified.thesis: Alumni Ventures provides accredited investors and institutions access to professionally-managed, diversified venture portfolios by co-investing alongside leading VC firms. The firm operates as a co-investor exclusively, not leading rounds or taking board seats, and focuses on democratizing venture capital access to individual investors.
verified.notes: Co-founder Luke Antal mentioned in source [3] but not identified as current senior partner on firm site. Sam Clayman from database entry not found in provided sources. No explicit sector focus list found in sources; database entry lists sectors but cannot be verified. SEC fined AV $700,000 in March 2022 for misleading fee statements and commingling funds (source [3]). Multiple fund products exist but no single 'flagship' fund name identified in sources.

changes.current: Alumni Ventures
changes.verified: AV

changes.current: Democratizing venture capital access through alumni networks. Dedicated deep tech and frontier fund investing alongside top-tier VCs. One of the most active VCs in the US by deal volume.

changesProposed.name: D1 Capital Partners

verified.name: D1 Capital Partners
verified.thesis: D1 Capital Partners invests in public and private markets globally. In private markets, the firm primarily takes later-stage non-controlling stakes in growth companies to support their expansion. The firm employs a global long/short equity strategy in public markets focusing on generating medium- to long-term returns.
verified.notes: AUM figure is from 2022 (most recent specific data in sources). Founded July 2018. Founder Daniel Sundheim is minority owner of Charlotte Hornets. D1 stands for 'Day One' referencing Amazon's concept. Spun out from Tiger Global (Tiger Cub fund).

changesProposed.name: In-Q-Tel

verified.name: In-Q-Tel
verified.thesis: In-Q-Tel identifies and invests in startups developing cutting-edge technologies to serve United States national security interests. The firm bridges the gap between technology needs of intelligence and defense communities and innovative capabilities of the private sector through technology development agreements and equity investments.
verified.notes: Not-for-profit 501(c)(3) corporation. Founded September 29, 1999 as 'Peleus', later known as 'In-Q-It' before becoming In-Q-Tel. Steve Bowsher appointed CEO on December 14, 2023, succeeding Christopher A. Darby. Office locations: Tysons VA (HQ), Menlo Park CA, Cambridge MA, London, Sydney, Singapore, Munich. Portfolio includes 50+ companies valued over $1B. Investments in 35+ U.S. states and 20+ countries. Primary government partners: CIA, DIA, FBI, NGA, NRO, NSA, DHS, U.S. Cyber Command, Office of Strategic Capital, U.K. and Australian national intelligence communities, U.S. Central Command, U.S. Space Force, U.S. Space Command, U.S. Army, Office of Strategic Intelligence & Analysis, National Nuclear Security Administration.

changes.current: Arlington, VA
changes.verified: Tysons, VA

changes.current: Strategic investments for CIA and US intelligence community. 800+ investments. Identifies and adapts commercial technologies for national security missions.
changes.verified: In-Q-Tel identifies and invests in startups developing cutting-edge technologies to serve United States national security interests. The firm bridges the gap between technology needs of intelligence and defense communities and innovative capabilities of the private sector through technology development agreements and equity investments.

changesProposed.notes: Sri Chandrasekar not found on current team page (source 1). AUM and flagship fund name not disclosed in sources. Since 2016 firm has backed more than 130 companies (source 0, 2). Team page lists Partners: Chris Morales, Tara Stokes, Ishan Sinha; other team members lack explicit titles. Point72 Ventures is a subsidiary of Point72 Asset Management (source 3).

changesProposed.name: Sequoia Capital

verified.name: Sequoia Capital
verified.thesis: Sequoia specializes in seed stage, early stage, and growth stage investments in private technology companies across clean tech, consumer internet, crypto, financial services, healthcare, mobile, and robotics sectors. The firm operates with an evergreen fund model allowing limited partners flexibility rather than traditional 10-year fund cycles.
verified.notes: In June 2023, Sequoia announced plans to separate into three independent entities: Sequoia (U.S. and Europe), Peak XV Partners (India and Southeast Asia), and HongShan (China), with separation expected to complete by March 2024. In November 2025, Alfred Lin and Pat Grady became joint managing partners succeeding Roelof Botha.

changes.current: Menlo Park, CA
changes.verified: Menlo Park, California

changesProposed.name: Shield Capital

verified.name: Shield Capital
verified.thesis: Shield Capital invests in early-stage companies building technologies that matter in artificial intelligence, autonomy, cybersecurity, and space. The firm focuses on the convergence of commercial technology and national security, with a team of veteran investors, founders, and national security leaders.
verified.notes: Founded year (2021), AUM ($500M+), and flagship fund (Shield Capital Fund II) from database entry could not be verified in provided sources. Wikipedia source [3] is about the defensive device 'shield', not the firm, and thus is not relevant. Current senior partners identified from firm team page: Philip Bilden and Raj Shah are listed as Founders and Managing Partners; Michael Brown and John Serafini are listed as Partners. Additional team members (Ray Rothrock, Jillian Manus, etc.) are listed as Venture Partners or lower roles and were excluded per instructions to list only current senior partners.

changes.current: Investing in the technologies and companies critical to national security. Led by former Pentagon DIU director Raj Shah and HarbourVest co-founder Philip Bilden. Deep defense and intelligence community networks.

The firm focuses on the convergence of commercial technology and national security, with a team of veteran investors, founders, and national security leaders.

changesProposed.name: Thrive Capital

verified.name: Thrive Capital
verified.thesis: Thrive Capital invests in software and internet companies. The firm has participated in notable investments across technology platforms and has expanded into growth and late-stage investing while maintaining early-stage investments.
verified.notes: Wikipedia lists Nitin Nohria as 'executive chair' (became firm's first executive chairman in 2022). Database entry lists 'Kareem Zaki' and 'Miles Grimshaw' as key partners, but these names do not appear in Wikipedia source. Thrive IX raised $5B total ($4B late-stage + $1B early-stage) announced August 2024. Thrive Capital Partners X raised $10B+ in 2026. Wikipedia sector focus is explicitly 'software and internet investments' only; database entry includes 'Defense & Security', 'AI & Software', 'Robotics & Automation' which are not supported by this source.

changesProposed.name: a16z (Andreessen Horowitz)

verified.name: Andreessen Horowitz
verified.thesis: a16z invests in seed to venture to growth-stage technology companies across multiple sectors including AI, bio + healthcare, consumer, crypto, enterprise, fintech, games, infrastructure, and companies building toward American dynamism. The American Dynamism fund supports companies building in hard tech sectors including space, defense, manufacturing, and robotics to serve national interest.
verified.notes: AUM figure of $90B+ is as of March 2026 per Wikipedia and April 30, 2026 per firm website. American Dynamism fund launched in 2023 with initial $600M capitalization. American Dynamism fund is managed by Katherine Boyle, David Ulevitch, and Erin Price-Wright per Wikipedia; database entry lists only Boyle and Ulevitch as key partners but Erin Price-Wright also manages the fund. Raghu Raghuram joined as general partner in October 2025.
